reasoning: The code exactly matches the finding description. In class-base-api-controller.php: verify_permissions() (line 57) calls current_user_can(Filters::apply_view_plugin_menu_capability_filters()) and verify_admin_permissions() (line 67) calls current_user_can(Filters::apply_connect_plugin_capability_filters()). In class-filters.php: apply_view_plugin_menu_capability_filters() returns apply_filters('leadin_view_plugin_menu_capability', 'edit_posts') (line 152) and apply_connect_plugin_capability_filters() returns apply_filters('leadin_connect_plugin_capability', 'manage_options') (line 159). LEADIN_PREFIX is defined as 'leadin' in leadin.php line 66, confirming the exact filter hook names. There is no secondary capability check, no hardcoded guard, and no sanitization of the returned capability string before it is passed to current_user_can(). Multiple REST endpoints under leadin/v1 are guarded only by these callbacks (register_leadin_route uses verify_permissions; register_leadin_admin_route uses verify_admin_permissions). If any installed plugin or theme registers add_filter('leadin_view_plugin_menu_capability', function(){ return 'read'; }), every authenticated subscriber passes verify_permissions(). The reachability is correctly stated as conditional on a third-party hook. No attacker-controlled HTTP input is needed — the downgrade occurs at PHP hook registration time. Severity 'medium' is appropriate: exploitable only under a specific co-condition, but no mitigating guard exists in the plugin itself.
